what_it_does: This file is a descriptive ledger that inventories the runtime-core area of the Company fabric. It catalogs eight core modules, summarizes their public surfaces and responsibilities, maps their dependencies, records live/dormant status, lists notable/surprising findings, and defines the design terms and audit laws used across the area.
observations: Catalogs 8 of 85 runtime files, totaling 19,318 lines read (some fully, some partially, one referenced).
suite.py is described as a 12,168-line stateless application layer with 50+ generic verb methods, but it does not spawn or resume sessions.
bridge.py is a stdlib http.server UI face with 150+ /api routes and no Flask/FastAPI.
session_supervisor.py is a separate service on 127.0.0.1:8771 and the sole spawner/writer of agent_sessions events.
brain_router.py routes questions to fleet, recall, or model sources, with model selection resolved dynamically by context size.
cc_channels.py abstracts two live-injection transports: HTTP POST to a session port and supervisor /inject for fork-owned sessions.
session_channels.py builds channels and gatherings from an append-only JSONL log using read-time folds and graph-locked seq numbers.
channel_boundary.py publishes shared-channel posts to Supabase but its Realtime subscriber is stubbed and pending builder wiring.
render_declaration.py is referenced as the R1.2 event layer imported by session_supervisor, but not analyzed in depth.
The file includes a dependency graph, a live/dormant table, 15 numbered notable findings, a peripheral-modules list, and a definitions section.
Repeated design laws include single-writer events, registry-is-truth, log-is-the-index, consent-not-lockdown, and fail-loud/teaching refusal.
Audit and synthesis citations (C6, B3, F-FIX-5, R1-R2.5, etc.) appear throughout as explicit references.
standouts: The runtime core is deliberately split: Suite reads/models, bridge serves UI, supervisor owns session fleet, brain routes questions, channels manage messaging.
session_supervisor.py enforces a single-writer law for agent_sessions events and is the only spawner of Claude Code subprocesses.
The channel fabric uses append-only JSONL logs and read-time folds rather than mutable state tables.
Brain model selection is dynamic (TIM-RULE) based on estimated context size, not a hardcoded default.
channel_boundary.py is live but incomplete: publish path is solid, Realtime subscribe/inject is stubbed.
conventions: Registry-is-truth: closed vocabularies and constants defined once (CHANNEL_OPS, MODES, NODE_STATES, etc.).
Log-is-the-index: append-only JSONL leaves are the source of truth, with state folded at read time.
Single-responsibility separation across read, serve, spawn, route, and transport layers.
Fail-loud/teaching posture: errors name the limit, env var, and how to recover rather than failing silently.
Consent-not-lockdown: wider capabilities are always available but require explicit operator consent signal.
Audit citations embedded in comments and module docstrings as explicit law references.
No hardcoded state-to-verb mappings; verb/mode specs live in dataclass registries.
Uses stdlib http.server for a large route table instead of a web framework.
Provider routing done via launcher command rather than environment overlay.
concerns: channel_boundary.py is marked live but incomplete; the Realtime subscriber transport is stubbed and depends on builder wiring.
render_declaration.py is only referenced, not read/analyzed, so its actual contract is not verified here.
suite.py and bridge.py are large enough that the ledger notes partial reads (offset/limit or route table only).
Coverage is 8/85 files, so many peripheral dependencies are listed but not described.
Several claims rely on external audit/synthesis documents not reproduced in this file.
The ledger is dated 2026-06-28 and may drift from the code it describes.
notes: A high-level, interpretive map of the runtime core that lists who does what, how the pieces connect, which parts are live or incomplete, and the design laws a reader must keep in mind.
questions: What do the remaining 77 runtime files contain, and how do they connect to these eight core modules?
What is the exact event contract and fan-out behavior of render_declaration.py?
How is the TIM-RULE model picker implemented in suite.py/cognition.py?
What is the pending Tim/lead product decision for the shared-vs-internal channel discriminator?
What is the current offline test status for the channel_boundary.py publish path?
How do the 150+ bridge routes map onto the 50+ Suite methods?
Where are the Mirror-Registry rules that derive spawn-flag posture, and what do they contain?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated as a 'Complete Inventory' of the runtime core engine. It is actually a summarized, interpretive catalog with module descriptions, dependency graph, status table, findings, and terminology — useful and structured, but not an exhaustive line-by-line inventory.
apparent_themes: Separation of concerns across read, serve, spawn, route, and transport.
Append-only logs as the single source of truth.
Operator-centric control with explicit capability boundaries.
intention_signals: The file is trying to make the runtime core navigable and trustworthy for builders and operators by showing clear module boundaries, live build status, surprising design choices, and remaining gaps, while reinforcing that the runtime is a federation of specialized services rather than one monolithic engine.
novelty: Unusual choices include a stdlib-only HTTP server for 150+ routes, held-open stdin stream-json injection for Claude Code sessions, append-only JSONL logs with read-time folds for cross-session fabric, dynamic brain model resolution, a separate session-supervisor service with a single-writer event law, registry-derived spawn-flag posture, and least-privilege Supabase principals for shared channels.
contribution: This file contributes a structured, interpretive index of the runtime-core area that a fused system would keep as living documentation: module boundaries, public surfaces, dependency graph, live/dormant status, design laws and terminology, and explicit build gaps such as the stubbed Realtime subscriber.
summary_for_embedding: A descriptive ledger inventorying eight core modules of the Company runtime-core area, their public APIs, dependencies, live/dormant status, notable design findings, and terminology, with coverage of 8 of 85 files.
intention_for_embedding: To provide a navigable, trustworthy map of the runtime core for builders and operators, highlighting module responsibilities, contracts, surprising choices, and remaining build gaps.
